title: PMBOK 8 Principles, Domains & Mindset
summary: The PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition structure - the project management mindset, six principles, seven performance domains, and the processes/Focus Areas - and how to use them as a reasoning lens on the PMP exam.

sections.heading: How PMBOK 8 is structured
sections.body: The PMP exam rewards judgement, not memorising one process map. PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition frames good project management as a mindset that drives the mechanics.

rows.value: Three dimensions plus six principles - how to think.
Seven areas that need sustained attention for success.
Forty processes with inputs, tools and outputs, organised by performance domain.
Five familiar lifecycle groupings (the former Process Groups), applied across predictive, agile and hybrid work.

blocks.title: Use it as a lens
lines: The best answer usually protects value, engages people well, fits the context, and responds to uncertainty in a disciplined but adaptable way.
Mindset drives mechanics: principles are how to think; domains are what to manage; processes and Focus Areas are how the work flows.

sections.heading: The mindset and the six principles
sections.body: PMBOK 8 describes a project management mindset built on three dimensions - proactive, ownership, and value-driven - expressed through six principles (a community-driven refinement of the Seventh Edition's twelve).

blocks.title: Three mindset dimensions
lines: Proactive.
Ownership.
Value-driven.

blocks.title: The six principles -> what each means

items.text: See the project as part of a wider system of interdependencies, stakeholders and organisational context (systems thinking).
Keep attention on outcomes and benefits, not just on completing activities or outputs.
Build quality into processes and deliverables rather than inspecting it in afterwards.
Lead with stewardship, ethics and ownership of outcomes; serve the team and stakeholders.
Weigh environmental, social and economic impact across the whole life cycle.
Create a collaborative, psychologically safe environment where the team can self-organise and perform.

blocks.title: Exam use
blocks.text: On the exam, the principles eliminate rigid, bureaucratic, reactive, or self-serving options.

sections.heading: The seven performance domains
sections.body: The performance domains describe what must be managed well for good outcomes. PMBOK 8 uses seven. Treat them as lenses for what to manage, not steps in sequence.

rows.value: Identify, engage and maintain productive relationships. Communications now sits inside this domain, not as a separate area.
Define and control the deliverables and the work to produce them.
Sequence and time the work, and keep it realistic.
Justify, fund, budget and control the money, and protect the business case.
Acquire, develop and manage the people and physical resources.
Set decision rights, oversight, alignment to strategy and change control.
Address threats and opportunities and the project's overall uncertainty.

blocks.title: Memory hook
blocks.text: Stakeholders, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Resources, Governance, Risk.

sections.heading: Processes and the five Focus Areas
sections.body: Unlike the Seventh Edition, PMBOK 8 reintroduces processes - about forty of them, each with inputs, tools and techniques, and outputs (ITTOs return), organised under the performance domains rather than the old knowledge-area grid.

blocks.title: The five Focus Areas (former Process Groups)
What Focus Areas are
lines: Renamed from Process Groups and framed as approach-agnostic - they apply whether delivery is predictive, agile or hybrid.
They are one lens on the work, not the whole model.
Focus Areas = the five Process Groups relabelled: lifecycle flow, not a rigid waterfall sequence.

sections.heading: What changed from PMBOK 7
sections.body: If you studied the Seventh Edition, note the differences so you are not caught out. The underlying mindset is continuous: value, stewardship, tailoring, and engaging people well still drive the best answers.

rows.value: Twelve condensed into six (a re-synthesis, not a subset).
Eight became seven, renamed and reorganised (Team and Planning concerns now sit within Resources, Scope, Schedule and Governance).
Folded into the Stakeholders domain - no longer a standalone area.
Return (~40 processes), absent from the principles-only Seventh Edition.
Appears in an appendix (automation, assistant, augmentation), not as a core domain.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: 'Six principles / seven domains / Focus Areas / ~40 processes' = PMBOK 8.
'Twelve principles / eight domains / no processes' = the older Seventh Edition.

sections.heading: Mapping to the ECO (People / Process / Business Environment)
sections.body: The PMP Examination Content Outline tests People, Process and Business Environment; the principles and domains support all three.

groups.label: Driven by principles
lines: Be an Accountable Leader.
Build an Empowered Culture.

groups.label: Driven by domains
lines: Stakeholders.
Resources.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Coaching, conflict resolution, engagement, servant leadership, team empowerment.

groups.label: Driven by principles
lines: Adopt a Holistic View.
Embed Quality.
Focus on Value.

groups.label: Driven by domains
lines: Scope, Schedule, Finance, Governance, Risk.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Choosing the approach, disciplined delivery, change control, measurement, managing uncertainty.

groups.label: Driven by principles
lines: Focus on Value.
Integrate Sustainability.
Adopt a Holistic View.

groups.label: Driven by domains
lines: Governance.
Finance.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Benefits realisation, compliance, strategic alignment, external impact.

blocks.title: Balance
blocks.text: The same answer can span domains; choose the option that best balances people, delivery discipline, and organisational value.

sections.heading: Reasoning to the best answer
sections.body: Work through these lenses in order to reach the strongest option.

items.title: Value and accountability
lines: Which option best protects the intended outcome, treats people and resources responsibly, and aligns with PMI's ethics?

items.title: The people dimension
lines: The strongest answer engages the right stakeholders, supports the team, addresses conflict directly, and uses influence before authority.
Options that ignore communication, bypass collaboration, or punish prematurely are usually weak.

items.title: Context and tailoring
lines: Predictive work may call for formal review and change control.
Adaptive work may call for reprioritising the backlog with the product owner and inspecting and adapting.
Hybrid respects both.

items.title: Quality, risk and uncertainty
lines: Strong answers assess impact before acting, tackle root causes, and avoid creating hidden risk.

items.title: A holistic view
lines: Consider the downstream effect on stakeholders, governance, delivery and long-term value.
The best PMP answer solves the immediate issue while preserving trust, alignment and sustainable delivery.

blocks.title: Reliable pattern
blocks.text: Assess first, engage the right people, use the approach that fits the context, and act to protect value.

source: Drawn from PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition, the PMP Examination Content Outline, the Agile Practice Guide, and the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct.
